content:
3 min read
Just now

--

July 20, 2026
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Everyone dreams about writing code on the first day of an internship.
I did too.
But reality was a little different — and honestly, I’m glad it was.
Instead of jumping straight into development, my first day was about understanding the tools, the workflow, and the habits that professional software engineers follow every single day.
Looking back, I realized these lessons are just as important as writing code.

The Beginning
Like every first day, mine started with document verification and getting familiar with the company.
Since no development tasks had been assigned yet, I decided to make the most of the time instead of simply waiting.
Rather than passively reading documentation, I created a small practice project and used ChatGPT as my mock team lead. The goal was simple: simulate a real developer workflow and practice everything I learned.
That turned out to be one of the best decisions of the day.

My First Real Git Workflow
Today, I finally practiced the complete Git workflow from start to finish.
Instead of only reading about Git commands, I actually used them.
I learned how to:

• Clone a repository

• Understand the difference between untracked and staged files

• Stage changes

• Create commits

• Push code to GitHub

• Set an upstream branch using git push -u

• Switch branches using git switch
Seeing the entire workflow in action made Git feel much less intimidating.
It finally started making sense.

A Small Problem I Faced
The hardest part wasn’t understanding Git.
It was remembering the commands.
At first, every command looked similar, and I found myself constantly checking my notes.
To solve this, I merged all my Git notes into a single PDF using I Love PDF so I could quickly refer to them whenever I got stuck.
But the biggest improvement came from repetition.
The more I practiced, the less I needed the notes.

Learning Good Development Habits
Today wasn’t just about Git commands.
It was also about learning why certain practices exist.
Two lessons stood out:
Never develop directly on the main branch.
and
Never commit your .venv folder to GitHub.
They seem like small rules, but they’re the kind of habits that make collaboration smoother and projects cleaner.
I’m glad I learned them early.

My First Look at Jira
Another new tool today was Jira.
I only received a basic introduction, but I now understand why development teams rely on it to organize work, assign tasks, and track progress.
I’m still curious about how teams use Jira in their everyday workflow, and that’s something I hope to explore over the coming weeks.

More Than Just Technology
One thing I enjoyed today was meeting the other interns.
Every internship is more than just learning new technologies.
It’s also about learning from the people around you, asking questions, and growing together.
I’m looking forward to those conversations just as much as the technical work.

What I Learned Today
Today’s biggest lesson wasn’t a Git command.
It was this:
Practice teaches faster than memorization.
Reading commands is one thing.
Using them in a realistic workflow is something completely different.
That simple realization made today feel productive, even without working on an actual company project.

Looking Ahead
No production code.
No bugs fixed.
No features shipped.
And that’s perfectly okay.
Every software engineer starts somewhere, and today I built the foundation that I’ll use throughout the rest of my internship.
Tomorrow will bring something new.
And when it does, I’ll be ready.
This article is part of my 365 Days of Becoming a Software Engineer series, where I document my journey from intern to engineer — one day, one lesson, and one challenge at a time.
